title: How Not to Rise to the Occasion
gist: Using a Lincoln Center retrospective of Italian historical cinema, Aaron Timms argues these films illuminate not heroism but the base instincts of political crisis (cowardice, compromise, self-deception) and reads them as a cautionary mirror for an America that resembles various eras of Italian upheaval.

insights.claim: Italian history is uniquely fertile in supplying parallels for the present United States, which sits between neo-fascist theatrics, constitutional stagnation, a Trumpian cult of personality, and an insurgent socialist left focused on state 'delivery.'
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Frames US political risk as oscillating between reaction and radical-competence revival; watch which Italian era (Risorgimento, interwar fascism, anni di piombo) the moment most resembles as a read on regime trajectory.
insights.quote: No country can match Italy’s fertility when it comes to supplying historical analogies for the state of America today, caught between neo-fascist theatrics, constitutional stagnation, the Trumpian cult of personality, and an insurgent socialist left intent on “delivery”
insights.locator: No country can match Italy's fertility

insights.claim: The applicability of specific historical analogies shifts rapidly, but Italian history reliably furnishes some instructive parallel to current US events at any given moment.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: A caution against over-fitting any single historical analogy to the present; treat analogy-matching as a fast-moving sentiment gauge, not a fixed forecast.
insights.locator: The suitability of these analogies may shift

insights.claim: Italian historical cinema's most illuminating subject is not political bravery but the baser instincts of crisis—cowardice, indifference, compromise, and self-deception—and the many ways it is possible to do nothing while history flies by.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Reframes the base case of political crisis as passive non-participation rather than heroic resistance—useful prior when forecasting how elites and publics actually behave under pressure.
insights.quote: these films vividly capture the many different ways in which it’s possible to do nothing during times of political crisis—to uncourageously sit on the sidelines and watch history fly by
insights.locator: But if you watch these films in succession

insights.claim: In Bertolucci's 1900, the industrial heir Alberto embodies a 'frozen liberal' caught between responsibility and desire but incapable of prosecuting either, inverting Yeats so that the worst, not the best, lack all conviction.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: A model of paralyzed centrism failing under extremist pressure—a template for how liberal establishments abdicate during polarization.
insights.quote: Unlike in the Yeats poem, here the worst, not the best, lack all conviction.

insights.claim: History in 1900 unfolds as a sequence of opportunities not taken and immobilizing tradeoffs between security and valor; its arc is long but bends toward more of the same.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: A pessimistic base rate—expect continuity and repetition rather than moral progress; a counter to arc-of-justice narratives.
insights.quote: Its arc is long but it bends toward more of the same.

insights.claim: Across Senso, Love and Anarchy, and The Leopard, revolutionary idealism repeatedly comes apart in the face of sexual desire, with the struggle for freedom playing out between the legs as much as on the streets.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Argues personal appetite reliably undercuts political resolve—a reminder that grand movements founder on private incentives and distraction.
insights.quote: Here revolutionary idealism comes apart in the face of sexual desire, and the struggle for freedom unfolds as much between the legs as on the streets.

insights.claim: The genius of Italian political cinema is taking history's nobodies seriously—the forgotten enablers, manipulators, and distracted would-be heroes without whom bigger events would not have reached their eventual scale.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Points to the causal weight of peripheral enablers in political outcomes—useful when modeling how minor actors' passivity or complicity amplifies major events.
insights.quote: The satirical genius of Italian political cinema is to take history’s nobodies seriously, to highlight the background cast of forgotten enablers
insights.locator: But in these films the times are always

themes.theme: Immaturity as the soil of fascism

insights.claim: Fellini's Amarcord advances the critique that interwar Italians were too immature, too preoccupied with petty intrigues, and too distractible to develop the moral responsibility needed to resist fascism's rise.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Locates a society's vulnerability to authoritarianism in cultural immaturity and distraction rather than overt ideology—a diagnostic to apply to distracted modern publics.
insights.quote: Italians were simply too immature, too preoccupied with petty intrigues, and too distractible to develop the moral responsibility to resist the rise of fascism
insights.locator: Part of the critique of interwar Italian society

insights.claim: Under fascism's fetish for hierarchy and rank, lesser social distinctions—between young and old, sane and hysterical, virginal and perverted—all lose their definitional clarity.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: Suggests authoritarian order paradoxically dissolves ordinary social categories—a subtle marker of regime encroachment beyond formal politics.

insights.claim: The Leopard's Tancredi captures the core quietism of Italian political cinema with the line that for things to stay the same, everything must change, transforming from redshirt radical into a law-and-order reactionary.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: The gattopardo principle—superficial revolution that preserves underlying power—is a durable frame for evaluating whether political upheaval actually shifts the distribution of power.
insights.quote: “For things to stay the same, everything must change,” Tancredi tells his uncle
insights.locator: The most memorably comic character arc

insights.claim: In this cinematic tradition, events and ideas may change but the underlying farce always stays the same, and allegiances are so provisional and revisable as to be essentially meaningless.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: A cynical prior on political loyalties and reform—treat professed allegiances as low-conviction and reversible, and expect structural continuity beneath rhetorical change.
insights.quote: Events and ideas may change, but the farce always stays the same.

insights.claim: In this filmic corpus the magnitude of historical events always runs up against the smallness of human desire.
insights.speaker: Aaron Timms
insights.so_what: A compact model: individual petty motives systematically blunt collective historical forces—useful when large-scale forecasts assume rational collective response.
insights.quote: the magnitude of events always runs up against the smallness of human desire
